metadata.summary: Efficient library for spectral analysis in high-energy astrophysics.

documentation:
# ELISA: Efficient Library for Spectral Analysis in High-Energy Astrophysics [![PyPI - Python Version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/astro-elisa?color=blue&logo=Python&logoColor=white&style=for-the-badge)](https://pypi.org/project/astro-elisa) [![PyPI - Version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/astro-elisa?color=blue&logo=PyPI&logoColor=white&style=for-the-badge)](https://pypi.org/project/astro-elisa) [![License: GPL v3](https://img.shields.io/github/license/wcxve/elisa?color=blue&logo=open-source-initiative&logoColor=white&style=for-the-badge)](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-3.0)
[![Coverage Status](https://img.shields.io/coverallsCoverage/github/wcxve/elisa?logo=Coveralls&logoColor=white&style=for-the-badge)](https://coveralls.io/github/wcxve/elisa) [![Documentation Status](https://img.shields.io/readthedocs/astro-elisa?logo=Read-the-Docs&logoColor=white&style=for-the-badge)](https://astro-elisa.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest) ``ELISA`` aims to provide a modern and efficient tool to explore and analyze the spectral data. It is designed to be user-friendly and flexible. The key features of ``ELISA`` include: - **Ease of Use**: Simple and intuitive interfaces - **Robustness**: Utilizing the state-of-the-art algorithm to fit, test, and compare models - **Performance**: Efficient computation backend based on [JAX](https://jax.readthedocs.io/en/latest/notebooks/quickstart.html) - ... **NOTE**: ``ELISA`` is currently under active development. Please be aware of potential brittleness, bugs, and changes to the API as the design evolves. ----- **Table of Contents** - [Installation](#installation) - [Documentation](#documentation) - [License](#license) ## Installation ### Stable Version It is recommended to install ``ELISA`` in a new [``conda``](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/stable/user-guide/getting-started.html) environment as follows: 1. Create a new ``conda`` environment. The following command creates a new environment named "elisa" with ``Python`` 3.11: ```console conda create -n elisa python=3.11 ``` Note that you can customize the environment name to your preference, and the ``Python`` version should range from 3.9 to 3.12. 2. Activate the environment we just created: ```console conda activate elisa ``` 3. Install ``ELISA`` using ``pip``: ```console pip install astro-elisa ``` ## Use ``Xspec`` Models If you want to use models from [Xspec](https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/xanadu/xspec/manual/Models.html), make sure ``HEASoft`` and ``Xspec v12.12.1+`` are installed on your system, and the ``HEASoft`` environment is initialized, then use the following command to install [``xspex``](https://github.com/wcxve/xspex): ```console pip install xspex ``` ### Development Version The latest version of ``ELISA`` can be installed by the following command: ```console pip install -U git+https://github.com/wcxve/elisa.git ``` ## Documentation Read the documentation at: https://astro-elisa.readthedocs.io ## License `ELISA` is distributed under the terms of the [GPL-3.0](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-3.0-standalone.html) license.

classes.name: elisa.models.add.Band
documentation:
Gamma-ray burst continuum developed by Band et al. (1993) [^footnote-1].

$$
N(E) = K \begin{cases} \bigl(\frac{E}{E_0}\bigr)^\alpha     \exp\bigl(-\frac{E}{E_\mathrm{c}}\bigr),     &\text{if } E < (\alpha-\beta) E_\mathrm{c}, \\\\ \bigl(\frac{E}{E_0}\bigr)^\beta \exp(\beta-\alpha)     \left[         \frac{(\alpha-\beta)E_\mathrm{c}}{E_0}     \right]^{\alpha-\beta},     &\text{otherwise,} \end{cases}
$$

where $E_0$ is the pivot energy fixed at 100 keV.

# Parameters

alpha

: The low-energy power law index $\alpha$, dimensionless.

beta

: The high-energy power law index $\beta$, dimensionless.

Ec

: The characteristic energy $E_\mathrm{c}$, in units of keV.

K

: The amplitude $K$, in units of ph cm⁻² s⁻¹ keV⁻¹.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

# References

[^footnote-1]: [Band, D., et al. 1993, ApJ, 413, 281](https://adsabs.harvard.edu/full/1993ApJ...413..281B)

classes.name: elisa.models.add.BandEp
documentation:
Gamma-ray burst continuum developed by Band et al. (1993) [^footnote-1],
parametrized by the peak of $\nu F_\nu$.

$$
N(E) = K \begin{cases} \bigl(\frac{E}{E_0}\bigr)^\alpha     \exp\left[-\frac{(2+\alpha)E}{E_\mathrm{p}}\right],     &\text{if } E < \frac{(\alpha-\beta)E_\mathrm{p}}{2+\alpha}, \\\\ \bigl(\frac{E}{E_0}\bigr)^\beta \exp(\beta-\alpha)     \left[         \frac{(\alpha-\beta)E_\mathrm{p}}{(2+\alpha)E_0}     \right]^{\alpha-\beta},     &\text{otherwise}, \end{cases}
$$

where $E_0$ is the pivot energy fixed at 100 keV.

:::{warning}
This model requires the low-energy power law index $\alpha$
to be greater than -2.0.
:::

# Parameters

alpha

: The low-energy power law index $\alpha$, dimensionless.

beta

: The high-energy power law index $\beta$, dimensionless.

Ep

: The peak energy $E_\mathrm{p}$ of $\nu F_\nu$,
  in units of keV.

K

: The amplitude $K$, in units of ph cm⁻² s⁻¹ keV⁻¹.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

# References

[^footnote-1]: [Band, D., et al. 1993, ApJ, 413, 281](https://adsabs.harvard.edu/full/1993ApJ...413..281B)

classes.name: elisa.models.add.Blackbody
documentation:
Blackbody function.

$$
N(E) = \frac{C K E^2}{(kT)^4 [\exp(E/kT)-1]},
$$

where $C=8.0525$ ph.

# Parameters

kT

: The temperature $kT$, in units of keV.

K

: The amplitude $K = L_{39}/D_{10}^2$, where $L_{39}$ is the
  source luminosity in units of 10³⁹ erg s⁻¹ and $D_{10}$ is the
  distance to the source in units of 10 kpc.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

classes.name: elisa.models.add.BlackbodyRad
documentation:
Blackbody function with normalization proportional to the surface area.

$$
N(E) = \frac{C K E^2}{\exp(E/kT)-1},
$$

where $C=1.0344 \times 10^{-3}$ ph cm⁻² s⁻¹ keV⁻³.

# Parameters

kT

: The temperature $kT$, in units of keV.

K

: The amplitude $K = R_\mathrm{km}^2/D_{10}^2$, where
  $R_\mathrm{km}$ is the source radius in km and $D_{10}$ is
  the distance to the source in units of 10 kpc.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

classes.name: elisa.models.add.PLPhFlux
documentation:
Power law function with photon flux used as normalization.

$$
N(E) &=     \mathcal{F}_\mathrm{ph}     \left[         \int_{E_\mathrm{min}}^{E_\mathrm{max}}         \left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)^{-\alpha} \, \mathrm{d}E     \right]^{-1}     \left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)^{-\alpha}\\     &=     \mathcal{F}_\mathrm{ph} (1 - \alpha)     \left[         \left(\frac{E_\mathrm{max}}{E_0}\right)^{1 - \alpha}         - \left(\frac{E_\mathrm{min}}{E_0}\right)^{1 - \alpha}     \right]^{-1}     \left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)^{-\alpha},
$$

where $E_0$ is the pivot energy fixed at 1 keV.

# Parameters

emin

: Minimum energy of the band to calculate the flux, in units of keV.

emax

: Maximum energy of the band to calculate the flux, in units of keV.

alpha

: Power law photon index $\alpha$, dimensionless.

F

: Photon flux $\mathcal{F}_\mathrm{ph}$, in units of ph cm⁻² s⁻¹.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

classes.name: elisa.models.add.PLEnFlux
documentation:
Power law function with energy flux used as normalization.

$$
N(E) &=     \mathcal{F}_\mathrm{en}     \left[         \int_{E_\mathrm{min}}^{E_\mathrm{max}}         \left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)^{-\alpha} \, E \, \mathrm{d}E     \right]^{-1}     \left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)^{-\alpha}\\     &=     \mathcal{F}_\mathrm{en} (2 - \alpha)     \left[         \left(\frac{E_\mathrm{max}}{E_0}\right)^{2 - \alpha}         - \left(\frac{E_\mathrm{min}}{E_0}\right)^{2 - \alpha}     \right]^{-1}     \left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right)^{-\alpha},
$$

where $E_0$ is the pivot energy fixed at 1 keV.

# Parameters

emin

: Minimum energy of the band to calculate the flux, in units of keV.

emax

: Maximum energy of the band to calculate the flux, in units of keV.

alpha

: Power law photon index $\alpha$, dimensionless.

F

: Energy flux $\mathcal{F}_\mathrm{en}$, in units of erg cm⁻² s⁻¹.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

classes.name: elisa.models.conv.PhFlux
documentation:
Normalize an additive model by photon flux between `emin` and `emax`.

$$
N'(E) =     \mathcal{F}_\mathrm{ph}     \left[\int_{E_\mathrm{min}}^{E_\mathrm{max}} N(E) \, dE\right]^{-1}     N(E)
$$

:::{warning}
The normalization of one of the additive components **must** be fixed
to a positive value.
:::

:::{warning}
The flux is calculated by trapezoidal rule, and is accurate only if
enough numbers of energy grids are used.
:::

# Parameters

emin

: Minimum energy of the band to calculate the flux, in units of keV.

emax

: Maximum energy of the band to calculate the flux, in units of keV.

F

: Photon flux $\mathcal{F}_\mathrm{ph}$, in units of ph cm⁻² s⁻¹.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

ngrid

: The energy grid number to use. The default is 1000.

elog

: Whether to use logarithmically regular energy grids.
  The default is True.

classes.name: elisa.models.conv.EnFlux
documentation:
Normalize an additive model by energy flux between `emin` and `emax`.

$$
N'(E) =     \mathcal{F}_\mathrm{en}     \left[\int_{E_\mathrm{min}}^{E_\mathrm{max}} EN(E)\,dE\right]^{-1}     N(E)
$$

:::{warning}
The normalization of one of the additive components **must** be fixed
to a positive value.
:::

:::{warning}
The flux is calculated by trapezoidal rule, and is accurate only if
enough numbers of energy grids are used.
:::

# Parameters

emin

: Minimum energy of the band to calculate the flux, in units of keV.

emax

: Maximum energy of the band to calculate the flux, in units of keV.

F

: Energy flux $\mathcal{F}_\mathrm{en}$, in units of erg cm⁻² s⁻¹.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

ngrid

: The energy grid number to use. The default is 1000.

elog

: Whether to use logarithmically regular energy grids.
  The default is True.

classes.name: elisa.models.conv.ZAShift
documentation:
Redshifts an additive model.

Consider a source with an emission area of radius $R$ at redshift
$z$. Given the flux function $N(E)$ [ph s⁻¹ cm⁻² keV⁻¹] at the
radius $R$, the observed number of photons $n$ between the
energy range $e_1$ [keV] and $e_2$ [keV] during an exposure
time of $\Delta t$ [s] is calculated as follows:

$$
n &= \frac{R^2}{{D_\mathrm{c}}^2} \frac{\Delta t}{1+z}      \int_{e_1(1+z)}^{e_2(1+z)} N(E) \, \mathrm{d}E   \\\\   &= \frac{R^2}{{D_\mathrm{c}}^2} \frac{\Delta t}{1+z}      \int_{E_1}^{E_2} N(E) \, \mathrm{d}E,
$$

where $E_1 = e_1 (1+z)$ [keV], $E_2 = e_2 (1+z)$ [keV] and
$D_\mathrm{c}$ is the comoving distance of the source at redshift
$z$.

Note that the $\frac{R^2}{{D_\mathrm{c}}^2}$ factor is absorbed into
the normalization of $N(E)$ in practice.

# Parameters

z

: Redshift $z$, dimensionless.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

classes.name: elisa.models.conv.ZMShift
documentation:
Redshifts a multiplicative model.

Consider a source at redshift $z$. Given the dimensionless model
function $M(E)$, the observed value between the energy range
$e_1$ [keV] and $e_2$ [keV] is calculated as follows:

$$
m &= \frac{1}{(e_2 - e_1)(1+z)}      \int_{e_1(1+z)}^{e_2(1+z)} M(E) \, \mathrm{d}E   \\\\   &= \frac{1}{E_2 - E_1} \int_{E_1}^{E_2} M(E) \, \mathrm{d}E,
$$

where $E_1 = e_1 (1+z)$ [keV] and $E_2 = e_2 (1+z)$ [keV].

# Parameters

z

: Redshift $z$, dimensionless.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

classes.name: elisa.models.conv.VAShift
documentation:
Velocity shifts an additive model.

Consider a source with an emission area of radius $R$, moving with
speed $v$ along line of sight. Given the flux function $N(E)$
[ph s⁻¹ cm⁻² keV⁻¹] at the radius $R$, the observed number of photons
$n$ between the energy range $e_1$ [keV] and $e_2$ [keV]
during an exposure time of $\Delta t$ [s] is calculated as follows:

$$
n &= \Delta t \int_{fe_1}^{fe_2} N(E) \, \mathrm{d}E   \\\\   &= \Delta t \int_{E_1}^{E_2} N(E) \, \mathrm{d}E,
$$

where $E_1 = f e_1$ [keV], $E_2 = f e_2$ [keV], and
$f = 1 - v/c$.

# Parameters

v

: Velocity $v$, in units of km s⁻¹.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

classes.name: elisa.models.conv.VMShift
documentation:
Velocity shifts a multiplicative model.

Consider a source moving with speed $v$ along line of sight. Given
the dimensionless model function $M(E)$, the observed value between
the energy range $e_1$ [keV] and $e_2$ [keV] is calculated as
follows:

$$
m &= \frac{1}{f (e_2 - e_1)} \int_{f e_1}^{f e_2} M(E) \, \mathrm{d}E   \\\\   &= \frac{1}{E_2 - E_1} \int_{E_1}^{E_2} M(E) \, \mathrm{d}E,
$$

where $E_1 = f e_1$ [keV], $E_2 = f e_2$ [keV], and
$f = 1 - v/c$.

# Parameters

v

: Velocity $v$, in units of km s⁻¹.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

classes.name: elisa.models.mul.Edge

Absorption edge.

classes.name: elisa.models.mul.ExpAbs
documentation:
Low-energy exponential rolloff.

$$
M(E) = \exp\left(-\frac{E_\mathrm{c}}{E}\right).
$$

# Parameters

Ec

: The e-folding energy $E_\mathrm{c}$ for the absorption,

latex

method

classes.name: elisa.models.mul.ExpFac
documentation:
Exponential modification.

$$
M(E) = \begin{cases} 1 + A \exp\bigl(-\frac{f E}{E_0}\bigr), &\text{if } E \ge E_\mathrm{c}, \\\\ 1, &\text{otherwise,} \end{cases}
$$

where $E_0$ is the pivot energy fixed at 1 keV.

# Parameters

A

: The amplitude of effect $A$, dimensionless.

f

: The exponential factor $f$, dimensionless.

Ec

: The start energy of modification $E_\mathrm{c}$, in units of keV.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

classes.name: elisa.models.mul.GAbs
documentation:
Gaussian absorption line.

$$
M(E) = \exp\left[             -\frac{\tau}{\sqrt{2\pi} \sigma}             \exp\left[                 -\frac{\left(E - E_\mathrm{l}\right)^2}{2 \sigma^2}             \right]         \right].
$$

The optical depth at line center is $\frac{\tau}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}$.

# Parameters

El

: The line energy $E_\mathrm{l}$, in units of keV.

sigma

: The line width $\sigma$, in units of keV.

tau

: The line depth $\tau$, in units of keV.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

classes.name: elisa.models.mul.HighECut

$$

# Parameters

Ec

: The cutoff energy $E_\mathrm{c}$, in units of keV.

Ef

: The e-folding energy $E_\mathrm{f}$, in units of keV.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

The photon cross-sections are obtained by interpolating cross-sections
table, which is the same as threeml does, except that if input energy
is outside the table energy range, extrapolated value is used.
:::

methods.name: elisa.models.mul.PhAbs.xsect_list
documentation: Get available photon cross-section list.

Photoelectric absorption.

$$
M(E) = \exp \left[ -\eta_\mathrm{H}\ \sigma(E) \right],
$$

where $\sigma(E)$ is the photo-electric cross-section, **NOT**
including Thomson scattering.

# Parameters

nH

: The equivalent hydrogen column density $\eta_\mathrm{H}$,
  in units of 10²² cm⁻².

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

abund

: Abundance table to use. Available options are:

  > - `'angr'` [^footnote-1] (Photospheric, using Table 2)
  > - `'aspl'` [^footnote-2] (Photospheric, using Table 1)
  > - `'feld'` [^footnote-3]
  > - `'aneb'` [^footnote-4]
  > - `'grsa'` [^footnote-5]
  > - `'wilm'` [^footnote-6]
  > - `'lodd'` [^footnote-7] (Photospheric, using Table 1)
  > - `'lpgp'` [^footnote-8] (Photospheric, using Table 4)
  > - `'lpgs'` [^footnote-8] (Proto-solar, using Table 10)

  The default is `'angr'`.

xsect

: Photon cross-section to use. Available options are:

  > - `'bcmc'` [^footnote-9] with a new He cross-section [^footnote-10]
  > - `'obcm'` [^footnote-9]
  > - `'vern'` [^footnote-11]

  The default is `'vern'`.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

# References

    197-214](<https://doi.org/10.1016/0016-7037(89)90286-X>)

methods.name: elisa.models.mul.TBAbs.abund_list
documentation: Get available abundance list.

The Tuebingen-Boulder ISM absorption model.

This model calculates the cross-sections for X-ray absorption by the ISM as
the sum of the cross-sections for X-ray absorption due to the gas-phase
ISM, the grain-phase ISM, and the molecules in the ISM.

# Parameters

nH

: The equivalent hydrogen column density $\eta_\mathrm{H}$,
  in units of 10²² cm⁻².

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

abund

: Abundance table to use. Available options are:

  > - `'angr'` [^footnote-1] (Photospheric, using Table 2)
  > - `'aspl'` [^footnote-2] (Photospheric, using Table 1)
  > - `'feld'` [^footnote-3]
  > - `'aneb'` [^footnote-4]
  > - `'grsa'` [^footnote-5]
  > - `'wilm'` [^footnote-6]
  > - `'lodd'` [^footnote-7] (Photospheric, using Table 1)
  > - `'lpgp'` [^footnote-8] (Photospheric, using Table 4)
  > - `'lpgs'` [^footnote-8] (Proto-solar, using Table 10)

  The default is `'angr'`.

xsect

: Always use cross-section `'vern'` [^footnote-9] as baseline.

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

# References

classes.name: elisa.models.mul.WAbs
documentation:
A photo-electric absorption using Wisconsin cross-sections [^footnote-1].

$$
M(E) = \exp \left[ -\eta_\mathrm{H}\ \sigma(E) \right],
$$

where $\sigma(E)$ is the photo-electric cross-section, **NOT**
including Thomson scattering.

:::{warning}
The {class}`WAbs` model is obsolete and is only included for comparison
with historical results. The {class}`TBAbs` model should be used for
the ISM or {class}`PhAbs` for general photoelectric absorption.
:::

# Parameters

nH

: The equivalent hydrogen column density $\eta_\mathrm{H}$,
  in units of 10²² cm⁻².

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the component. Defaults to class name.

abund

: Always use abundance table `'aneb'` [^footnote-2].

xsect

: Always use Wisconsin cross-sections [^footnote-1].

method

: Numerical integration method. Defaults to 'trapz'.

classes.name: elisa.models.parameter.ConstantInterval

methods.name: elisa.models.parameter.ConstantInterval.method
documentation: Numerical integration method.

Constant parameter to be integrated over a given interval.

When assigning {class}`ConstantInterval` parameters to a model component,
the model will be evaluated according to the following formula:

$$
\frac{1}{\prod_i (b_i - a_i)} \int f(E, \vec{\theta}(\vec{p}, \vec{q})) \, \mathrm{d} \vec{p}
$$

where $f$ is the model function, $\vec{\theta}$ is the
parameter vector of the model, $\vec{p}$ is the
{class}`ConstantInterval` parameters, $\vec{q}$ is the other
parameters, and $a_i$ and $b_i$ are the intervals given
by $\vec{p}$.

# Parameters

name: str

: Parameter name.

interval: array_like

: The interval, a 2-element sequence.

method

: Numerical integration method used to integrate over the parameter.
  Available options are:

  > - `'quadgk'`: global adaptive quadrature by Gauss-Konrod rule
  > - `'quadcc'`: global adaptive quadrature by Clenshaw-Curtis rule
  > - `'quadts'`: global adaptive quadrature by trapz tanh-sinh rule
  > - `'romberg'`: Romberg integration
  > - `'rombergts'`: Romberg integration by tanh-sinh
  >   (a.k.a. double exponential) transformation

  The default is `'quadgk'`.

latex

: $\LaTeX$ format of the parameter. The default is as `name`.

kwargs

: Extra kwargs passed to integration methods. See [^footnote-1] for details.

# References

[^footnote-1]: [quadax docs](https://quadax.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#adaptive-integration-of-a-callable-function-or-method)

methods.name: elisa.models.model.CompiledModel.flux
documentation:
Calculate the flux of model between `emin` and `emax`.

:::{warning}
The flux is calculated by trapezoidal rule, which may not be
accurate if not enough energy bins are used when the difference
between `emin` and `emax` is large.
:::

# Parameters

emin

: Minimum value of energy range, in units of keV.

emax

: Maximum value of energy range, in units of keV.

params

: Parameter sequence or mapping for the model.

energy

: When True, calculate energy flux in units of erg cm⁻² s⁻¹;
  otherwise calculate photon flux in units of ph cm⁻² s⁻¹.
  The default is True.

comps

: Whether to return the result of each component. The default is
  False.

ngrid

: The energy grid number to use in integration. The default is 1000.

log

: Whether to use logarithmically regular energy grid. The default is
  True.

# Returns

jax.Array, or dict[str, jax.Array]

: The model flux.

spec_back_scale

back_area_scale

back_back_scale

channel

: Channel type. The default is 'Ch'.

params

: Parameter sequence or mapping for the model.

name

: Name of the simulation data. The default is 'simulation'.

seed

: Random seed for simulation. The default is 42.

kwargs

: Additional keyword arguments passed to {class}`ObservationData`.

functions.name: elisa.infer.likelihood.pgstat_background
documentation:
Optimized background for PG-statistics given estimate of source counts.

:::{note}
The optimized background here is always non-negative, which differs
from XSPEC [^footnote-1].
:::

# Parameters

s

: Estimate of source counts.

n

: Observed counts (source and background).

b_est

: Estimate of background counts.

b_err

: Uncertainty of background counts.

a

: Exposure ratio between source and background observations.

# Returns

JAXArray

: The profile background.

# References

[^footnote-1]: [XSPEC Manual Appendix B: Statistics in XSPEC](https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/xanadu/xspec/manual/XSappendixStatistics.html).

methods.name: elisa.infer.fit.Fit._parse_input
documentation: Check if data, model, and stat are correct and return lists.

Abstract base class for model fitting.

# Parameters

data

: The observation data.

model

: The model used to fit the data.

stat

: The likelihood option for the data and model. Available likelihood
  options are:

  > - `'chi2'`: Gaussian data
  > - `'cstat'`: Poisson data
  > - `'pstat'`: Poisson data with known background
  > - `'pgstat'`: Poisson data with Gaussian background
  > - `'wstat'`: Poisson data with Poisson background

  The default is None, which means automatically choosing the suitable
  likelihood options for the datasets and models.

seed

: Seed of random number generator used for fit. The default is 42.

methods.name: elisa.infer.fit.MaxLikeFit.mle
documentation:
Search Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for the model.

# Parameters

init

: Initial guess for the maximum likelihood estimation. The default is
  as parameters' default values.

method

: Optimization algorithm used to find the MLE.
  Available options are:

  > - `'minuit'`: Migrad algorithm of {mod}`iminuit`.
  > - `'lm'`: Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm of {mod}`optimistix`.
  > - `'ns'`: Nested sampling of {mod}`jaxns`. This option first
  >   search MLE globally, then polish it with local minimization.

  The default is 'minuit'.

# Other Parameters

max_steps

: The maximum number of steps the solver can take. The default is
  131072\.

throw

: Whether to report any failures of the solver. Defaults to True.

verbose

: Whether to print fit progress information. The default is False.

# Returns

MLEResult

: The MLE result.

methods.name: elisa.infer.fit.BayesFit.nuts
documentation:
Run the No-U-Turn Sampler of {mod}`numpyro`.

:::{note}
If the chains are not converged well, see ref [^footnote-1] for more
information on how to finetune the NUTS sampler.
:::

# Parameters

warmup

: Number of warmup steps.

steps

: Number of steps to run for each chain.

chains

: Number of MCMC chains to run. If there are not enough devices
  available, chains will run in sequence. Defaults to the number of
  `jax.local_device_count()`.

init

: Initial parameter for sampler to start from.

chain_method

: The chain method passed to {class}`numpyro.infer.MCMC`.

progress

: Whether to show progress bar during sampling. The default is True.

nuts_kwargs

: Extra parameters passed to {class}`numpyro.infer.NUTS`.

# Returns

PosteriorResult

: The posterior sampling result.

# References

[^footnote-1]: NumPyro tutorial: [Bad posterior geometry and how to deal with
    it](https://num.pyro.ai/en/stable/tutorials/bad_posterior_geometry.html)

methods.name: elisa.infer.fit.BayesFit.jaxns
documentation:
Run the Nested Sampler of {mod}`jaxns`.

:::{note}
Parameters `s`, `k`, and `c` are defined in the paper [^footnote-1].
For more information of the sampler parameters, see ref [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2].
:::

# Parameters

max_samples

: Maximum number of posterior samples. The default is 131072.

num_live_points

: Approximate number of live points. The default is `c` * (`k` + 1).

s

: Number of slices per dimension. The default is 5.

k

: Number of phantom samples. The default is 0.

c

: Number of parallel Markov chains. The default is 30 * `D`, where
  `D` is the dimension of model parameters. It takes effect only
  for num_live_points=None.

devices

: Devices to use. Defaults to all available devices.

difficult_model

: If True, uses more robust default settings (`s` = 10 and
  `c` = 50 * `D`). It takes effect only for `num_live_points` = None,
  `s` = None or `c` = None. Defaults to False.

parameter_estimation

: If True, uses more robust default settings for parameter estimation
  (`k` = `D`). It takes effect only for `k` = None.
  Defaults to False.

verbose

: Print progress information. The default is False.

term_cond

: Termination conditions for the sampling. The default is as in
  {class}`jaxns.TermCondition`.

ns_kwargs

: Extra parameters passes to {class}`jaxns.DefaultNestedSampler`.

# Returns

PosteriorResult

: The posterior sampling result.

# References

[^footnote-1]: [Phantom-Powered Nested Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.11330)

[^footnote-2]: [JAXNS API doc](https://jaxns.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/jaxns/index.html#jaxns.DefaultNestedSampler)

methods.name: elisa.infer.fit.BayesFit.ultranest
documentation:
Run the Nested Sampler of {mod}`ultranest`.

# Parameters

ess

ignore_nan

: Whether to transform a NaN log probability to a large negative
  number (-1e300). The default is True.

  :::{warning}

  with model computation.
  :::

constructor_kwargs

: Extra parameters passed to
  {class}`ultranest.ReactiveNestedSampler`.

termination_kwargs

  {class}`ultranest.ReactiveNestedSampler.run()`.

read_file

: Read the log file from a previous run. The dictionary should
  contain the log directory and other optional parameters. It
  should be noted that when providing this keyword argument, the
  sampler will not run, but read the log file instead, and make
  sure the data and model setting is the same as the previous run.

methods.name: elisa.infer.fit.BayesFit.aies
documentation:
Affine-Invariant Ensemble Sampling (AIES) of {mod}`numpyro`.

Affine-invariant ensemble sampling [^footnote-1] is a gradient-free method
that informs Metropolis-Hastings proposals by sharing information
between chains. Suitable for low to moderate dimensional models.
Generally, num_chains should be at least twice the dimensionality
of the model.

:::{note}
This kernel must be used with even `num_chains` > 1 and
`chain_method='vectorized'`.
:::

# Parameters

warmup

: Number of warmup steps.

steps

: Number of steps to run for each chain.

chains

: Number of MCMC chains to run. Defaults to 4 * `D`, where `D` is
  the dimension of model parameters.

init

: Initial parameter for sampler to start from.

chain_method

: Available options are `'vectorized'` and `'parallel'`.
  Defaults to `'vectorized'`.

n_parallel

: Number of parallel chains to run when `chain_method` is
  `"parallel"`. Defaults to `jax.local_device_count()`.

progress

: Whether to show progress bar during sampling. The default is True.
  If `chain_method` is set to `'parallel'`, this is always False.

moves

: Moves for the sampler.

aies_kwargs

: Extra parameters passed to {class}`numpyro.infer.AIES`.

# Returns

PosteriorResult

: The posterior sampling result.

# References

[^footnote-1]: *emcee: The MCMC Hammer*
    (<https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1086/670067>),
    Daniel Foreman-Mackey, David W. Hogg, Dustin Lang,
    and Jonathan Goodman.

methods.name: elisa.infer.fit.BayesFit.ess
documentation:
Ensemble Slice Sampling (ESS) of {mod}`numpyro`.

Ensemble Slice Sampling [^footnote-1] is a gradient free method
that finds better slice sampling directions by sharing information
between chains. Suitable for low to moderate dimensional models.
Generally, num_chains should be at least twice the dimensionality of
the model.

:::{note}
This kernel must be used with even `num_chains` > 1 and
`chain_method='vectorized'`.
:::

# Parameters

warmup

: Number of warmup steps.

steps

: Number of steps to run for each chain.

chains

: Number of MCMC chains to run. Defaults to 4 * `D`, where `D` is
  the dimension of model parameters.

init

: Initial parameter for sampler to start from.

chain_method

: Available options are `'vectorized'` and `'parallel'`.
  Defaults to `'vectorized'`.

n_parallel

: Number of parallel chains to run when `chain_method` is
  `"parallel"`. Defaults to `jax.local_device_count()`.

progress

: Whether to show progress bar during sampling. The default is True.
  If `chain_method` is set to `'parallel'`, this is always False.

moves

: Moves for the sampler.

ess_kwargs

: Extra parameters passed to {class}`numpyro.infer.ESS`.

# Returns

PosteriorResult

: The posterior sampling result.

# References

[^footnote-1]: zeus: a PYTHON implementation of ensemble slice sampling
    for efficient Bayesian parameter inference
    (<https://academic.oup.com/mnras/article/508/3/3589/6381726>),
    Minas Karamanis, Florian Beutler, and John A. Peacock.

functions.name: elisa.infer.helper.get_helper
documentation: Get helper functions for fitting.
def get_reparam(dist: Distribution) -> tuple[Reparam, Callable] | None:
"""Get reparam for a distribution."""
# TODO: support more reparameterizers
if isinstance(dist, (Normal, StudentT, Cauchy)):
param = '_centered'
suffix = '_decentered'
reparam = LocScaleReparam()
elif isinstance(dist, TransformedDistribution):
suffix = '_base'
reparam = TransformReparam()
transforms = dist.transforms
def inv(x):
"""Inverse transformation."""
for t in reversed(transforms):
x = t.inv(x)
return x
elif isinstance(dist, ProjectedNormal):
suffix = '_normal'
reparam = ProjectedNormalReparam()
elif isinstance(dist, VonMises):
suffix = '_unwrapped'
reparam = CircularReparam()
else:
return None
return reparam, inv

classes.name: elisa.data.ogip.Data
documentation:
Handle observation data in OGIP standards [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2].

Load the observation spectrum, the telescope response and the possible
background, and handle the grouping of spectrum and response.

# Parameters

erange

: Energy range of interest in keV, e.g., `erange=[(0.5, 2), (5, 200)]`.

specfile

: Spectrum file path. For type II pha file, the row specifier must be
  given in the end of path, e.g., `specfile='spec.pha2{1}'`.

backfile

: Background file path. Read from the `specfile` header if None.
  For type II pha file, the row specifier must be given in the end of
  path, e.g., `backfile='back.pha2{1}'`.

respfile

: Response file path. Read from the `specfile` header if None.
  The path must be given if `RESPFILE` is undefined in the header.

ancrfile

: Ancillary response path. Read from the `specfile` header if None.

# Other Parameters

name

: Data name. Read from the `specfile` header if None. The name must
  be given if `DETNAM`, `INSTRUME` and `TELESCOP` are all
  undefined in the header.

group

: Method to group spectrum and background adaptively, these options are
  available so that each channel group has:

  > - `'const'`: `scale` number channels
  > - `'min'`: total (source + background) counts >= `scale`
  > - `'sig'`: source significance >= `scale` sigma
  > - `'bmin'`: background counts >= `scale`, used to avoid bias when
  >   using `wstat` to simultaneously fit the source and background
  > - `'bsig'`: background significance >= `scale` sigma, used to
  >   avoid bias when using `pgstat` to simultaneously fit the source
  >   and background
  > - `'opt'`: optimal binning, see Kaastra & Bleeker (2016) [^footnote-3]
  > - `'optmin'`: optimal binning with total counts >= `scale`
  > - `'optsig'`: optimal binning with source significance >= `scale`
  >   sigma
  > - `'optbmin'`: optimal binning with background counts >= `scale`
  > - `'optbsig'`: optimal binning with background significance
  >   \>= `scale` sigma

  The default is None.

scale

: Grouping scale for the method specified in `group`.

spec_poisson

: Whether the spectrum data follows counting statistics, reading from
  the `specfile` header. This value must be set if `POISSERR` is
  undefined in the header.

back_poisson

: Whether the background data follows counting statistics, reading
  from the `backfile` header. This value must be set if `POISSERR`
  is undefined in the header.

ignore_bad

: Whether to ignore channels with `QUALITY` being 2 or 5.
  The default is True. The possible values for spectral `QUALITY` are

  > - `0`: good
  > - `1`: defined bad by software
  > - `2`: defined dubious by software
  > - `5`: defined bad by user
  > - `-1`: reason for bad flag unknown

keep_channel_info

: Whether to keep channel information in the label of grouped
  channel. Takes effect only if `group` is not None or spectral data
  has `GROUPING` defined. The default is False.

sparse_response

: Whether the response matrix is sparse. The default is False.

# Notes

Reading and applying correction to data is not yet supported.

# References

[^footnote-1]: [The OGIP Spectral File Format](https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/docs/heasarc/ofwg/docs/spectra/ogip_92_007/ogip_92_007.html)

[^footnote-2]: [The Calibration Requirements for Spectral Analysis (Definition of
    RMF and ARF file formats)](https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/docs/heasarc/caldb/docs/memos/cal_gen_92_002/cal_gen_92_002.html)
    and [Addendum: Changes log](https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/docs/heasarc/caldb/docs/memos/cal_gen_92_002a/cal_gen_92_002a.html)

[^footnote-3]: [Kaastra & Bleeker 2016, A&A, 587, A151](https://doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/201527395)

methods.name: elisa.infer.nested_sampling.NestedSampler.diagnostics
documentation:
Plot diagnostics of the result. This is a wrapper of {func}`jaxns.plotting.plot_diagnostics`
and {func}`jaxns.plotting.plot_cornerplot`.

(EXPERIMENTAL) A wrapper for [jaxns](https://github.com/Joshuaalbert/jaxns) ,
a nested sampling package based on JAX.

See reference [1] for details on the meaning of each parameter.
Please consider citing this reference if you use the nested sampler in your research.

:::{note}
To enumerate over a discrete latent variable, you can add the keyword
`infer={"enumerate": "parallel"}` to the corresponding `sample` statement.
:::

:::{note}
To improve the performance, please consider enabling x64 mode at the beginning
of your NumPyro program `numpyro.enable_x64()`.
:::

**References**

1. *JAXNS: a high-performance nested sampling package based on JAX*,
   Joshua G. Albert (<https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.15286>)

```
param callable model

a call with NumPyro primitives

param dict constructor_kwargs

additional keyword arguments to construct an upstream
 instance.

param dict termination_kwargs

keyword arguments to terminate the sampler. Please
refer to the upstream  method.
```

**Example**

```
.. doctest::

    >>> from jax import random
    >>> import jax.numpy as jnp
    >>> import numpyro
    >>> import numpyro.distributions as dist
    >>> from numpyro.contrib.nested_sampling import NestedSampler

    >>> true_coefs = jnp.array([1., 2., 3.])
    >>> data = random.normal(random.PRNGKey(0), (2000, 3))
    >>> labels = dist.Bernoulli(logits=(true_coefs * data).sum(-1)).sample(random.PRNGKey(1))
    >>>
    >>> def model(data, labels):
    ...     coefs = numpyro.sample('coefs', dist.Normal(0, 1).expand([3]))
    ...     intercept = numpyro.sample('intercept', dist.Normal(0., 10.))
    ...     return numpyro.sample('y', dist.Bernoulli(logits=(coefs * data + intercept).sum(-1)),
    ...                           obs=labels)
    >>>
    >>> ns = NestedSampler(model)
    >>> ns.run(random.PRNGKey(2), data, labels)
    >>> samples = ns.get_samples(random.PRNGKey(3), num_samples=1000)
    >>> assert jnp.mean(jnp.abs(samples['intercept'])) < 0.05
    >>> print(jnp.mean(samples['coefs'], axis=0))  # doctest: +SKIP
    [0.93661342 1.95034876 2.86123884]
```

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.MLEResult.covar
documentation:
Calculate covariance matrix.

# Parameters

params

: Parameters to calculate covariance matrix. If not specified,
  calculate for parameters of interest.

fn

: A dict containing functions to calculate the covariance matrix.
  The keys are the names of the function results, and the values are
  the functions whose input is a dict of model parameters.

method

: Method used to calculate covariance. Available options are:

  > - `'hess'`: inverse of Hessian matrix from Minuit
  > - `'boot'`: calculate covariance based on bootstrap samples,
  >   {meth}`MLEResult.boot` must be called before using this
  >   method.

  The default is `'hess'`.

parallel

: Whether to evaluate `fn` in parallel when `method` is `'boot'`.
  The default is True.

# Returns

ParamsCovar

: The covariance matrix.

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.MLEResult.ci
documentation:
Calculate confidence intervals.

# Parameters

cl

: Confidence level for the confidence interval. If 0 < `cl` < 1, the
  value is interpreted as the confidence level. If `cl` >= 1, it is
  interpreted as the number of standard deviations. For example,
  `cl=1` produces a 1-sigma or 68.3% confidence interval.
  The default is 1.

params

: Parameters to calculate confidence intervals. If not specified,
  calculate for parameters of interest.

fn

: A dict containing functions to calculate the confidence intervals.
  The keys are the names of the function results, and the values are
  the functions whose input is a dict of model parameters.

method

: Method used to calculate confidence. Available options are:

  > - `'profile'`: use Minos algorithm of Minuit to find the
  >   confidence intervals based on the profile likelihood
  > - `'boot'`: use parametric bootstrap method to calculate
  >   the confidence intervals. {meth}`MLEResult.boot` must be
  >   called before using this method.

  The default is `'profile'`.

rtol

: The relative tolerance in determining the value of composite
  parameters and `fn` when `method` is `'profile'`.
  The default is 1e-6.

parallel

: Whether to evaluate `fn` in parallel when `method` is `'boot'`.
  The default is True.

# Returns

ConfidenceInterval

: The confidence intervals.

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.MLEResult.flux
documentation:
Calculate the flux of model.

:::{warning}
The flux is calculated by trapezoidal rule, and is accurate only
if enough numbers of energy grids are used.
:::

# Parameters

emin

: Minimum value of energy range, in units of keV.

emax

: Maximum value of energy range, in units of keV.

energy

: When True, calculate energy flux in units of erg cm⁻² s⁻¹;
  otherwise calculate photon flux in units of ph cm⁻² s⁻¹.
  The default is True.

cl

: Confidence level for the confidence interval. If 0 < `cl` < 1, the
  value is interpreted as the confidence level. If `cl` >= 1, it is
  interpreted as the number of standard deviations. For example,
  `cl=1` produces a 1-sigma or 68.3% confidence interval.
  The default is 1.

method

: Method used to calculate confidence. Available options are:

  > - `'profile'`: use Minos algorithm of Minuit to find the
  >   confidence intervals based on the profile likelihood
  > - `'boot'`: use parametric bootstrap method to calculate the
  >   confidence intervals. {meth}`MLEResult.boot` must be called
  >   before using this method.

  The default is `'profile'`.

ngrid

: The energy grid number to use in integration. The default is 1000.

# Other Parameters

comps

: Whether to return the result of each component. The default is
  False.

log

: Whether to use logarithmically regular energy grid. The default is
  True.

params

: Parameters dict to overwrite the fitted parameters.

# Returns

MLEFlux

: The flux of the model.

: Parameters dict to overwrite the fitted parameters. Ignored when
  `method` is `'profile'`.

cosmo

: Cosmology model used to calculate luminosity. The default is
  Planck18.

# Returns

PosteriorEiso

: The isotropic emission energy of the model.

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.PosteriorResult.ppc
documentation:
Perform posterior predictive check.

# Parameters

n

: The number of posterior predictions. The default is 10000.

seed

: The seed of random number generator used in posterior predictions.

parallel

: Whether to run simulation fit in parallel. The default is True.

n_parallel

: Number of parallel processes to use when `parallel` is `True`.
  Defaults to `jax.local_device_count()`.

progress

: Whether to display progress bar. The default is True.

update_rate

: The update rate of progress bar. The default is 50.

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.PosteriorResult.rhat
documentation:
Computes split R-hat over MCMC chains.

In general, only fully trust the sample if R-hat is less than 1.01. In
the early workflow, R-hat below 1.1 is often sufficient. See [^footnote-1] for
more information.

# References

[^footnote-1]: <https://arxiv.org/abs/1903.08008>

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.PosteriorResult.waic
documentation:
The widely applicable information criterion (WAIC).

Estimates the expected log point-wise predictive density (elpd) using
WAIC. Also calculates the WAIC's standard error and the effective
number of parameters. See [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-2] for more information.

# References

[^footnote-1]: <https://arxiv.org/abs/1507.04544>

[^footnote-2]: <https://arxiv.org/abs/1004.2316>

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.PosteriorResult.loo
documentation:
Pareto-smoothed importance sampling leave-one-out cross-validation
(PSIS-LOO-CV).

Estimates the expected log point-wise predictive density (elpd) using
PSIS-LOO-CV. Also calculates LOO's standard error and the effective
number of parameters. For more information, see [^footnote-1], [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

# References

[^footnote-1]: <https://avehtari.github.io/modelselection/CV-FAQ.html>

[^footnote-2]: <https://arxiv.org/abs/1507.04544>

[^footnote-3]: <https://arxiv.org/abs/1507.02646>

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.PosteriorResult.lnZ
documentation: Log model evidence and uncertainty.

methods.name: elisa.infer.results.PosteriorResult._loo_expectation
documentation:
Computes weighted expectations using the PSIS weights.

Notes
-----
The expectations estimated assume that the PSIS approximation is
working well. A small Pareto k estimate is necessary, but not
sufficient to give reliable estimates.

Parameters
----------
values : DataArray
    Values to compute the expectation.
data : str
    The data name.

Returns
-------
DataArray
    The expectation of the values.

modules.name: elisa.util.integrate
documentation: Numerical integration.

functions.name: elisa.util.integrate.make_integral_factory
documentation:
Get integral factory over the interval.

# Parameters

param_id

: Parameter ID.

interval: array_like

: The interval, a 2-element sequence.

method

: Numerical integration method used to integrate over the parameter.
  Available options are:

  > - `'quadgk'`: global adaptive quadrature by Gauss-Konrod rule
  > - `'quadcc'`: global adaptive quadrature by Clenshaw-Curtis rule
  > - `'quadts'`: global adaptive quadrature by trapz tanh-sinh rule
  > - `'romberg'`: Romberg integration
  > - `'rombergts'`: Romberg integration by tanh-sinh
  >   (a.k.a. double exponential) transformation

  The default is `'quadgk'`.

kwargs

: Extra kwargs passed to integration methods. See [^footnote-1] for details.

# Returns

integral_factory

: Given a model function, the integral factory outputs a new model
  function with the interval parameter being integrated out.

# References

[^footnote-1]: [quadax docs](https://quadax.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#adaptive-integration-of-a-callable-function-or-method)

methods.name: elisa.data.base.ObservationData.group
documentation:
Group the spectrum.

# Parameters

method

: Method to group spectrum and background adaptively, these options
  are available so that each channel group has:

  - `'const'`: `scale` number channels
  - `'min'`: total (source + background) counts >= `scale`
  - `'sig'`: source significance >= `scale` sigma
  - `'bmin'`: background counts >= `scale`, used to avoid bias when
    using `wstat` to simultaneously fit the source and background
  - `'bsig'`: background significance >= `scale` sigma, used to
    avoid bias when using `pgstat` to simultaneously fit the source
    and background
  - `'opt'`: optimal binning, see Kaastra & Bleeker (2016) [^footnote-1]
  - `'optmin'`: optimal binning with total counts >= `scale`
  - `'optsig'`: optimal binning with source significance >= `scale`
    sigma
  - `'optbmin'`: optimal binning with background counts >= `scale`
  - `'optbsig'`: optimal binning with background significance
    \>= `scale` sigma

scale

: Grouping scale.

# Warns

GroupWarning

: Warn if grouping scale is not met for any channel.

# Notes

If there are ignored channels in a channel group, this may cause an
inconsistency in a spectral plot. That is to say, the error bar of a
channel group will cover these bad channels, whilst these bad channels
are never used in fitting.

# References

[^footnote-1]: [Kaastra & Bleeker 2016, A&A, 587, A151](https://doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/201527395)

methods.name: elisa.data.base.ResponseData.fwhm
documentation:
Estimated Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) in photon energy space.

:::{note}
The calculation code is translated from `heasp`. This does assume
that the response has a well-defined main peak and operates by the
simple method of stepping out from the peak in both directions till
the response falls below half the maximum. A better solution would
obviously be to fit a gaussian.
:::

methods.name: elisa.data.base.ResponseData.channel_fwhm
documentation:
Estimated Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) in channel energy space.

:::{note}
The calculation code is translated from `heasp`. The calculation
interpolates the `estimated_fwhm` using the nominal channel energy
to give the FWHM for each channel. Assuming that FWHM does not
change significantly over the channel, so just find the FWHM at the
center energy of the channel.
:::

Response matrix data.

Parameters
----------
photon_egrid : array-like
    Photon energy array of response matrix, must be increasing.
channel_emin : array-like
    Left edge of the measurement channel energy array, must be increasing.
channel_emax : array-like
    Right edge of the measurement channel energy array, must be increasing.
response_matrix : array-like or sparse matrix
    Response matrix, the shape is (len(photon_egrid), len(channel)). This
    can be a sparse matrix.
channel : array-like
    Measurement channel information.
channel_type : str, optional
    Measurement channel type, e.g. `'PI'`. The default is 'Ch'.
sparse : bool, optional
    Whether the response matrix is sparse. The default is False.

methods.name: elisa.plot.scale.LinLogScale.get_transform
documentation: Return the `.LinLogTransform` associated with this scale.

The linear-logarithmic scale is logarithmic above positive `lin_thresh`
and linear otherwise.

# Parameters

base

: The base of the logarithm.

lin_thresh

: Defines the range `(lin_thresh, +inf)`, within which the plot is
  logarithmic.

lin_scale

: This allows the linear range `(-inf, lin_thresh)` to be stretched
  relative to the logarithmic range. Its value is the number of decades
  to use for the linear range. For example, when *lin_scale* == 1.0, the
  space used for `[0, lin_thresh]` range will be equal to one decade
  in the logarithmic range.

subs

: Where to place the subticks between each major tick.
  For example, in a log10 scale: `[2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]` will place
  8 logarithmically spaced minor ticks between each major tick.

functions.name: elisa.plot.util.gaussian_kernel_smooth
documentation:
Apply Gaussian kernel regression to data and then interpolate it.

:::{note}
The regression here is also known as Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression
[^footnote-1]. This helper function is adapted from [^footnote-2].
:::

# Parameters

x, y

: Arrays of x- and y-coordinates of data. Must be 1d and have the same
  length.

sigma

: Standard deviation of the Gaussian to apply to each data point. Larger
  values yield a smoother curve.

x_eval

: Array of x-coordinates at which to evaluate the smoothed result. The
  default is `x`.

null_thresh

: For evaluation points far from data points, the estimate will be
  based on very little data. If the total weight is below this threshold,
  return np.nan at this location. Zero means always return an estimate.
  The default of 0.6 corresponds to approximately one sigma away from
  the nearest datapoint.

# Returns

smoothed

: Smoothed data at `x_eval`.

# References

[^footnote-1]: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kernel_regression>

[^footnote-2]: <https://stackoverflow.com/a/61394682>

functions.name: elisa.plot.residuals.quantile_residuals_poisson
documentation:
Normalized quantile residuals for fit of Poisson data.

# Parameters

k

: Data value.

lam

: Model value.

keep_sign

: Whether to keep sign of the residuals as `sign(k - lam)`. The default
  is False.

random

: Whether to add random noise so that residuals are normally distributed.

seed

: Random seed to use in adding noise. The default is 42.

# Returns

ndarray

: The quantile residuals.

# Notes

When `random` is True, the residuals are theoretically guaranteed to be
normally distributed. If `keep_sign` is also True, then the normality of
the residuals may not be preserved. The recommended use is only `random`
or `keep_sign` being True.

# References

[^footnote-1]: doi:10.2307/1390802

[^footnote-2]: doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0181790

[^footnote-3]: doi:10.1111/j.1541-0420.2009.01191.x

[^footnote-4]: doi:10.1186/s12874-020-01055-2

functions.name: elisa.plot.residuals.quantile_residuals_poisson_poisson
documentation:
Normalized quantile residuals for joint fit of two Poisson data.

:::{note}
The calculation is based on Monte Carlo simulation. It is also possible
to calculate the quantile residuals by inverting the Cornish-Fisher
expansion. See the following link for more details:

> - <https://stats.stackexchange.com/a/73070>
> - <https://www.value-at-risk.net/the-cornish-fisher-expansion/>
> - <https://faculty.washington.edu/ezivot/econ589/ssrn-id1997178.pdf>
:::

# Parameters

k1, k2

: The first and second data values.

lam1, lam2

: The first and second model values.

ratio

: Background ratio.

random

: Whether to add random noise so that residuals are normally distributed.

seed

: Random seed to use in adding noise and simulation. The default is 42.

nsim

: The number of simulations to generate. The default is 10000.

# Returns

tuple of ndarray

: The quantile residuals, and flags to mark if the residuals are lower or
  upper limit.

# Parameters

idata

: The number of bins to use in histograms, either as a fixed value for

labels

: Labels to be displayed in axis label.

color

: Color to use for the plot.

divergences

: Whether to mark diverging samples.

fig

: The figure containing the corner plot.

Plotting configuration.
def _get_pit_pdf(pit_intervals: NumPyArray) -> NumPyArray:
"""Get the pdf of PIT.
References
assert len(pit_intervals.shape) == 2 and pit_intervals.shape[1] == 2
grid = np.unique(pit_intervals)
if grid[0] > 0.0:
grid = np.insert(grid, 0, 0)
if grid[-1] < 1.0:
grid = np.append(grid, 1.0)
n = len(pit_intervals)
mask = pit_intervals[:, 0] != pit_intervals[:, 1]
cover_mask = np.bitwise_and(
pit_intervals[:, :1] <= grid[:-1],
grid[1:] <= pit_intervals[:, 1:],
pdf = np.zeros((n, len(grid) - 1))
pdf[cover_mask] = np.repeat(
1.0 / (pit_intervals[mask, 1] - pit_intervals[mask, 0]),
np.count_nonzero(cover_mask[mask], axis=1),
idx = np.clip(grid.searchsorted(pit_intervals[~mask, 0]) - 1, 0, None)
pdf[~mask, idx] = 1.0 / (grid[idx + 1] - grid[idx])
return pdf.mean(0)
